The unsupervised anomaly detection experiment using autoencoders on streaming IoT sensor data has a fundamental category error: the design applies supervised evaluation methodology (stratified split with fault labels, precision at top-K, MTTF evaluation) to a model framed as unsupervised. If fault labels from maintenance logs are available for training, the problem is semi-supervised or supervised anomaly detection, not unsupervised. True unsupervised anomaly detection trains exclusively on normal operation data without label access, and the evaluation requires a separate labeled set that the model has never seen during training. The design mixes these paradigms without acknowledging the tension.

Mean time to failure (MTTF) as a primary evaluation metric is problematic for a batch experimental design. MTTF measures the expected operational time before equipment failure, which is a property of the equipment degradation process, not of the model. What the model can affect is detection lead time (how far in advance of actual failure the anomaly is flagged) and false positive rate. The design should use detection lead time, precision at K anomalies, and false positive rate as primary metrics rather than MTTF, which is not directly under model control.

The stratified random split on 12 months of streaming IoT sensor data is the standard temporal validity problem. Sensor degradation is a temporal process — readings from the days before a fault have systematic patterns that differ from normal operation, and randomly splitting this data will allow pre-fault readings to appear in training while the fault event appears in test (or vice versa), creating direct leakage through the temporal structure of degradation signals.

The threshold-based baseline comparison is appropriate for the domain, but the design states 'both methods undergo equivalent tuning.' Threshold-based systems are typically tuned to control false positive rate, not to optimize MTTF directly. The equivalence of tuning effort between an autoencoder and a threshold system should be specified more carefully to avoid comparing a well-optimized neural system against an undertuned threshold baseline.
all_issues_raised: Category error: the model is framed as unsupervised but fault labels from maintenance logs are used for evaluation stratification and metric computation; this is semi-supervised detection, not unsupervised, and the design conflates these paradigms

MTTF as primary metric measures equipment degradation rate, not model detection quality; detection lead time, precision at K anomalies, and false positive rate are the appropriate model performance metrics
Stratified random split on 12 months of streaming sensor data creates temporal leakage through pre-fault degradation patterns that systematically precede fault events

Threshold-based baseline tuning criterion is not specified — thresholds are typically tuned to control false positive rate, not to optimize the same objective as the autoencoder, making 'equivalent tuning' comparison unclear

Autoencoder reconstruction threshold selection (the decision boundary between normal and anomalous) is not specified; threshold choice substantially affects precision-recall trade-off and must be addressed for the model to be evaluable
all_issues_adjudicated: Category error: the model is framed as unsupervised but fault labels from maintenance logs are used for evaluation stratification and metric computation; this is semi-supervised detection, not unsupervised, and the design conflates these paradigms

MTTF as primary metric measures equipment degradation rate, not model detection quality; detection lead time, precision at K anomalies, and false positive rate are the appropriate model performance metrics
Stratified random split on 12 months of streaming sensor data creates temporal leakage through pre-fault degradation patterns that systematically precede fault events

Threshold-based baseline tuning criterion is not specified — thresholds are typically tuned to control false positive rate, not to optimize the same objective as the autoencoder, making 'equivalent tuning' comparison unclear

Autoencoder reconstruction threshold selection (the decision boundary between normal and anomalous) is not specified; threshold choice substantially affects precision-recall trade-off and must be addressed for the model to be evaluable

critic_raw:
The NLP model for student dropout prediction experiment contains several significant methodological concerns. The most serious is the oversampling strategy: the design states 'dropout instances will be oversampled in the training data' but does not specify whether oversampling is applied before or after the train/validation/test split. If the original instances are duplicated before splitting, validation and test sets may contain duplicates of training instances, producing optimistic and biased performance estimates. Oversampling should be applied only within the training fold, after all splits are determined on the original data.

The BERT-based model trained on 'interaction text data' from an online learning platform (forum posts, assignment submissions, course access logs) raises a fundamental input modality question: BERT is designed for natural language text, but many student interaction logs are structured event sequences (page views, quiz attempts, video timestamps) rather than natural language. The design does not specify what text is actually being processed by BERT — if it is forum posts and discussion content, this is a natural language application; if it is structured interaction logs, BERT is not the appropriate architecture.

The justified rationale for random splitting — 'temporal autocorrelation is weak relative to the strategic necessity to balance dropout instances' — conflates two separate problems. Balancing class representation is a training data concern (addressed by oversampling) that does not require abandoning temporal validity in evaluation. A chronological split can be used for train/test splitting while oversampling handles class imbalance within training. The justification conflates these independent design decisions.

The design mentions that exogenous factors like 'course revisions are partly mitigated by integrating related covariates, although not wholly eliminable.' Course revision periods in online learning platforms create natural distributional shifts — a student's dropout risk before and after a course redesign reflects different pedagogical environments. Without explicit handling (e.g., course version as a stratification variable or temporal period indicator), these shifts constitute an uncontrolled confound that could systematically affect model performance.
all_issues_raised: Oversampling of dropout instances may have been applied before data splitting, which would contaminate validation and test sets with duplicates of training examples and inflate performance estimates

BERT is applied to 'interaction text data' but the actual text modality is not specified — forum posts are natural language (appropriate for BERT), while structured event logs (page views, quiz attempts) are not and require different architectures

Random split justification conflates class imbalance handling (a training concern addressed by oversampling) with evaluation validity (a split design concern); these are independent decisions and the former does not justify the latter

Course revision periods create distributional shifts in dropout risk that are acknowledged but not adequately controlled — course version or curriculum period should be a stratification variable, not merely an included covariate

The AUPRC primary metric with oversampling in training but not necessarily in evaluation may be inconsistent — AUPRC on the original class distribution is more informative of deployment performance than on an oversampled distribution
all_issues_adjudicated: Oversampling of dropout instances may have been applied before data splitting, which would contaminate validation and test sets with duplicates of training examples and inflate performance estimates

BERT is applied to 'interaction text data' but the actual text modality is not specified — forum posts are natural language (appropriate for BERT), while structured event logs (page views, quiz attempts) are not and require different architectures

Random split justification conflates class imbalance handling (a training concern addressed by oversampling) with evaluation validity (a split design concern); these are independent decisions and the former does not justify the latter

Course revision periods create distributional shifts in dropout risk that are acknowledged but not adequately controlled — course version or curriculum period should be a stratification variable, not merely an included covariate

The AUPRC primary metric with oversampling in training but not necessarily in evaluation may be inconsistent — AUPRC on the original class distribution is more informative of deployment performance than on an oversampled distribution
